channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: How Algeria Is Navigating the War in Iran
item.description: Algiers has historically close ties with Tehran—but it may benefit from the ongoing war.

item.title: Trump Mulls Pulling U.S. Out of NATO
item.description: However, U.S. law dictates that only an act of Congress or a Senate resolution can withdraw Washington from the alliance.

item.title: Nepal’s New Leaders Go on the Offensive
item.description: Former officials were arrested over the weekend in connection with a crackdown on protesters last year.

item.title: Afghans Fear for Safety as War Returns
item.description: As the world watches Iran, Pakistan and Afghanistan clash.

item.title: Donald Trump’s approval rating has sunk to Joe Biden’s lowest point
item.description: He is mirroring his predecessor’s post-debate slump of 2024

item.title: What a U.S. Operation to Get Iran’s Uranium Would Look Like
item.description: It’s even more complicated than capturing a Venezuelan president.

item.title: Waiting for the War to End in Tehran
item.description: For Iranians under fire, surges of patriotism and criticism of the government.

item.title: Tehran Is Setting a Trap for Trump
item.description: Iran’s new leaders are more radical and less risk-averse than their predecessors.

item.title: Tehran Is Setting a Trap for Trump
item.description: Iran’s new leaders are more radical and less risk-averse than their predecessors.

item.title: The Kyrgyzneftegaz Case: Shairbek Tashiev Arrested
item.description: Shairbek, who resigned from parliament last month, had previously been questioned as a “witness" in the Kyrgyzneftgas case.

item.title: The Graveyard of Rohingya Diplomacy: Is Bangladesh Stuck in an ‘Exhaustion Trap’?
item.description: For almost a decade now, the gap between official progress and political reality has stalled Rohingya diplomacy.

item.title: Why Europe Is Unlikely to Face an Inflation Surge
item.description: In 2021-22, the European Central Bank failed to pay sufficient heed to mounting inflationary pressures, leading to a delayed response that made matters much worse. While today’s energy-price shock remains less acute than the one caused by Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the ECB appears committed to a more proactive response.

item.title: Cobalt, Congo, and China: Battery Power as Political Power
item.description: Insights from Nicholas Niarchos.

item.title: Iran War Opens New Markets for Kazakhstan
item.description: With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, Bangladesh is in a dire strait of its own and looking far afield for new energy partners, like Kazakhstan.

item.title: How Will South Korea Respond to the Iran-US Conflict?
item.description: Amid U.S. requests for naval involvement in the Strait of Hormuz, Seoul has three options: leveraging, minimizing, or compensating.

item.title: Can China Grow From Within?
item.description: At a time of intensifying geopolitical volatility, China’s embrace of a consumption-led model is not only about rebalancing growth, but also about anchoring it more firmly at home. Domestic demand offers insulation from external shocks, and along with developed capital markets, it can go a long way toward strengthening autonomy.

item.title: America’s foes see opportunity in Asia’s oil shock
item.description: Russia and China are quietly stepping in

item.title: Why women, more than men, are abandoning rural Japan
item.description: Bigger cities are, in turn, skewing more female

item.title: How the Gulf’s war is becoming Asia’s crisis too
item.description: Prices, debt, and scarcity will strike a blow against the world’s workshop

item.title: Scientists are working on “everything vaccines”
item.description: A single jab could protect against a wide range of pathogens

item.title: A trio of firms want to clean up steelmaking
item.description: The industry is currently responsible for around 8% of the world’s carbon emissions

item.title: Mark Carney’s party is poised to take control of Parliament
item.description: Canada’s fate will probably soon be squarely in Liberal hands

item.title: Might Hungary’s election sweep away MAGA’s favourite foreign leader?
item.description: Whether Viktor Orban wins or loses, the world’s populists and their opponents are watching

item.title: Lessons for the world from tiny Hungary
item.description: A regime loved by MAGA may soon lose power. That matters

item.title: How Africa is changing Catholicism
item.description: The world’s biggest church is becoming less Eurocentric but more unruly

item.title: Demand for autism care is soaring. The system is struggling to cope
item.description: A surge in diagnoses has created a booming industry, riddled with incentives that drive up costs and distort care

item.title: From MAHA to haha
item.description: The paranoid style of medicine is becoming a political liability

item.title: The war with Iran has blown up an America First policy
item.description: What country can afford both a war and the Jones Act?

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
A final favour Macron could do for France
item.description: A gracefully timed exit would reset France’s awkward electoral calendar

item.title: On the front lines, Russian soldiers pay officers to stay alive
item.description: The war against Ukraine has created a corrupt economy of blood money

item.title: Will the European Union’s next member come from the north?
item.description: Worried about trade and security, Iceland and Norway are warming to the EU

item.title: A revolution is coming for Germany’s intelligence services
item.description: Derided at home and abroad, Germany’s spooks are about to get sweeping new powers

item.title: How Fox News is luring in Gen Z
item.description: American boomers’ favourite TV network is trying new digital tricks

item.title: The hidden currency of office life
item.description: How status shapes motivation, conflict and career choices

item.title: India’s oil refiners are feeling the squeeze from the Gulf war
item.description: The conflict, and the government, are depleting margins

item.title: War with Iran could accelerate Africa’s oil revival
item.description: Favourable geology and new technologies make it an attractive place to drill

item.title: How the Department of Justice became a feeding ground for MAGA lobbyists
item.description: Allegations are swirling of an influence-peddling operation

item.title: Senegal’s government denies the gravity of its debt crisis
item.description: Its refusal to consider an IMF plan may make things worse

item.title: Iran’s permissive party islands contain hidden dangers
item.description: If American troops turn up, they should not expect a package holiday

item.title: Iran is taking a surprising toll of key American systems
item.description: Losses would be much higher in a war with China

item.title: Iran’s opposition in exile is rethinking its support for the war
item.description: The longer the war drags on, the less clear are the benefits for ordinary Iranians

item.title: The war in Iran is nearing a crossroads
item.description: With a Trump-imposed deadline looming, a relative lull in the fighting cannot last much longer

item.title: How would American ground forces take Kharg?
item.description: America is capable of seizing the island. But it would be immensely risky

item.title: Iran has damaged a surprising amount of American kit
item.description: Losses would be much higher in a war with China

item.title: Between Cooperation and Control: China’s Expanding Role in Central America
item.description: The key question is not whether China is “exporting authoritarianism,” but how its cooperation mechanisms interact with domestic dynamics already moving in that direction.

item.title: Aijan Sharshenova on the Japarov-Tashiev Political Divorce in Kyrgyzstan
item.description: Put one way, the Japarov-Tashiev split is a “political divorce… involving high stakes, such as significant assets, complex business structures, or intense custody battles.”

item.title: Indonesia’s UNIFIL Dilemma: Don’t Hand the US and Israel a Victory by Proxy
item.description: Withdrawing from Lebanon would undermine Indonesia’s peacekeeping credibility and ironically serve the very powers Jakarta claims to resist.

item.title: Will Kharg Island Decide the Future of US Alliances?
item.description: As confidence in the United States has eroded, allies have begun to hedge their bets by not automatically aligning themselves with America in the face of new crises. The US-Israeli war on Iran has thrown this dynamic into sharp relief, revealing a fundamental new constraint on American power.

item.title: How China hopes to win from the war
item.description: Never interrupt your enemy when he is making a mistake

item.title: The Iran war hurts China less than its rivals but more than it admits
item.description: One upside could be a reluctant change in its growth model

item.title: Why America, Not Iran, Has a Succession Problem
item.description: The United States is fighting Iran—a state that built institutional continuity into its founding architecture—with strategic assumptions borrowed from more personalistic regimes, where decapitation can work. Ironically, under President Donald Trump, the US is reshaping its own political system in that direction.

item.title: How worried should you be about private credit?
item.description: Its humbling could raise borrowing costs

item.title: Private-credit funds are showing signs of strain
item.description: Some panicky investors are running for the exit

item.title: “Liberation Day” has reshaped trade—but not as Donald Trump hoped
item.description: In many ways, global commerce has strengthened

item.title: Can a country get too rich?
item.description: Norway shows the potential pitfalls of uncommon prosperity

item.title: Trump’s Iran War Is a Dilemma, Not a Debacle
item.description: Each of Washington’s imperfect options comes with trade-offs and uncertainties.

item.title: Cuba in Free Fall
item.description: In the space of just a few weeks, Cuba’s external energy supply and main sources of foreign earnings have been cut off. With economic conditions rapidly deteriorating and many industries grinding to a halt, social unrest is mounting, posing a potential threat to the island’s governability.

item.title: Wars Fought for Fun Cannot Be Won
item.description: Many commentators have tried to divine a policy justification for the US war in Iran. But the simple explanation is that US President Donald Trump and US Secretary of “War” (Defense) Pete Hegseth attacked the Islamic Republic because they could, and because they take pleasure in killing or dominating other people.

item.title: The Third Islamic Republic
item.description: A war’s unintended consequences—for Iran, the Middle East, and the global order.

item.title: America Is Losing the Innovation Race
item.description: Why the future of science might be Chinese.

item.title: A Post-American Persian Gulf?
item.description: The Iran war will accelerate the region’s economic transformation.

item.title: Thailand’s Brittle Defense Against Oil Shocks
item.description: The country is in a better position than its immediate neighbors, but the coming weeks are set to test Prime Minister Anutin's new administration.

item.title: Why Laos’ Communist Party Chose Continuity
item.description: With the economy still in a fragile state, the ruling Lao People's Revolutionary Party has decided against significant changes at the top.

item.title: China, the Philippines, and the Real Lesson of Second Thomas Shoal
item.description: The Provisional Understanding emerged because coercion failed to accomplish Chinese objectives at acceptable costs to Beijing.

item.title: Index providers should not bend the rules for Elon Musk
item.description: They will only expose ordinary investors to unnecessary risks

item.title: Will Conflict in the Middle East Boost China’s Renewable Energy Sector?
item.description: The war in Iran has had a profound impact on global energy markets. Could China’s long-standing renewable ambitions take a leap forward as investors look for opportunities amid the volatility?

item.title: Coal is back in fashion
item.description: An LNG crunch is good news for the world’s dirtiest fuel

item.title: Ko Wen-je’s Downfall Strengthens the KMT’s Position Ahead of Key Elections
item.description: If the TPP ultimately fails to survive this crisis, the DPP may benefit in the long run. But in the near term, the KMT has the most to gain.

item.title: Our prediction for how SCOTUS will rule on birthright citizenship
item.description: Will the Trump administration lose another high-profile case?
